# **\[WIP\] Data Infra Vision and Strategy: Spark as a Service \[FY26Q3\]**

Status: *In progress*  Author: [Yi Chen](mailto:yichen@stripe.com)    Created: Aug 27, 2025  Last Updated: Aug 27, 2025

# Executive Summery

The Data Infrastructure team provides value to Slack by enabling a stable, low-cost Spark infrastructure on top of AWS EMR. We support different user cohorts like engineers, data analysts, data scientists. These users run approximately 30k to 40k unique Spark applications per day[\[1\]](#bookmark=id.8t08tlrix23n). As of September, 2025, we operate a fleet of \_\_\_ vcores, which costs about $\_\_\_ per year. We have ODCR of \~120 r8g.24xlarge EC2 instances for all critical workloads, and the usage ratio often dips below 50%. We are currently migrating from Spark 3.3 and 3.4 to Spark 3.5.5 (EMR 7.10.0).

Despite the significant improvement for cost and reliability, we understand that the current state is suboptimal. Our users are seeking a managed solution that abstracts away the complexity of infrastructure management while still granting them fine control over dependency, access controls, and other functionalities. 

To address these concerns, we are focusing on offering a more comprehensive and user-friendly solution that allows our users to concentrate on their core tasks and responsibilities without having to worry about underlying infrastructure management. 

We expect the following shift to incrementally occur in the next 2-4 years, assuming we do not have additional headcount.

# About this doc

In a complex environment, any widely adapted infrastructure faces uncertainty about what the future holds. New requirements (e.g. data sharing with Salesforce) pose challenges to existing trade-offs and are often addressed through a process of incremental development and continuous learning. This doc reflects this reality: we explicitly prioritize areas of exploration along with execution and we’re planning to review it at least semi-annually to incorporate new learnings. So this doc is a snapshot of our vision that includes all known and known unknowns at time of writing.

# Vision / Strategy

## Cluster Layout North Star

1. We aim to have only one large EMR cluster (because today at steady state we use less than 100 EC2 machines across all prod clusters)  
2. We aim to automate the auditable capacity management (to prevent unmanaged onboarding of risky workload, to reduce waste from ODCR and bursty critical workload)  
3. We aim to have static clusters only, and cluster rotation only happens when absolutely necessary (because ODCR is static, autoscaling means waste)  
4. We aim to leverage AI for cluster health monitor, anomaly detection and potentially automated tuning, automated capacity management.

## Managed Spark Capacity

Current State  
Currently users have a preference for dedicated clusters for their own workload, due to the vague boundary between reliability of the infrastructure and the reliability of the Spark applications in a multi-tenant system. A side effect of this is that increasing capacity becomes a de facto first response when jobs are at risk of missing SLA. The lack of Spark fundamental knowledge contributes to the following major categories of issues:

1. Cluster-level resource fragmentation causes large waste. We not only have team-specific clusters, we also have dedicated backfill clusters for teams. I estimate that we are wasting 30% of compute resources due to small EMR clusters autoscaling independently.  
2. Scheduled workloads with relaxed SLAs compete with workloads with higher SLA needs for resources. This results in resource inefficiencies.  
3. Lack of engineering rigar, resulted in waste from scaling up clusters in response to user ask without appropriate RCA and follow-ups.

## Cost

We waste about \_\_% of YARN capacity that is about $\_\_ a year. 

## Deployment Safety

## Security

## Development Experience

We don’t have much data on user feedback of development experiences from direct escalations, but the friction is obvious from the organizational level: teams are very reluctant to staff and prioritize Spark related projects, including job reliability improvements and major migrations. This is especially true as we face large migration from EMR 6 to EMR 7, and potentially migration over to Spark-on-Kubernetes.

North Star

1. Spark experimentation and prototyping is low friction.  
2. Debugging  
3. Better log retention  
4. Testing

Strategy  
Gear toward fixed cluster by disabling autoscaling. This keeps the machines alive and allow longer log retention.

## Operational Excellence

Spark application tuning and optimization is a challenge that is difficult to scale. Auto-tuning is a buzzword with little known success stories. LinkedIn mostly rely on Dr. Elephant to identify tuning issues with strict threshold for onboarding jobs in production cluster. Airbnb also has a heavy process that “certifies” critical data pipelines twice a year to ensure they remain in production quality.

This includes applications running on EMR, and a smaller number of applications that have been migrated to Kubernetes.
